Musical artist

**Randall Hank Williams** (born May 26, 1949), known professionally as **Hank Williams Jr.** or **Bocephus**, is an American singer-songwriter and musician. His musical style has been described as a blend of [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"), [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"), and [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"). He is the son of country musician [Hank Williams](/wiki/Hank_Williams "Hank Williams") and the father of musicians [Sam Williams](/wiki/Sam_Williams_(singer) "Sam Williams (singer)"), [Holly Williams](/wiki/Holly_Williams_(musician) "Holly Williams (musician)") and [Hank Williams III](/wiki/Hank_Williams_III "Hank Williams III"), and the grandfather of [Coleman Williams](/wiki/Coleman_Williams "Coleman Williams"). He is also the half-brother of [Jett Williams](/wiki/Jett_Williams "Jett Williams").

Williams began his career following in his famed father's footsteps, covering his father's songs and imitating his father's style. Williams' first television appearance was in a December 1963 episode of *[The Ed Sullivan Show](/wiki/The_Ed_Sullivan_Show "The Ed Sullivan Show")*, in which at the age of fourteen he sang several songs associated with his father. The following year, he was a guest star on *[Shindig!](/wiki/Shindig! "Shindig!")*.[[1]](#cite_note-2)[[2]](#cite_note-3)

As Williams struggled to define his own voice and place within the [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") genre, his style began slowly to evolve. His career was interrupted by a near-fatal fall while he was climbing [Ajax Peak in Montana](/wiki/List_of_mountains_in_Beaverhead_County,_Montana "List of mountains in Beaverhead County, Montana") on August 8, 1975.[[3]](#cite_note-People-4)[[4]](#cite_note-TheFall-5) After an extended recovery, he rebuilt his career in both the [country rock](/wiki/Country_rock "Country rock") and [outlaw country](/wiki/Outlaw_country "Outlaw country") scenes.[[5]](#cite_note-Burns-6) As a multi-instrumentalist, Williams' repertoire of musical instrument skills includes guitar, bass guitar, [upright bass](/wiki/Upright_bass "Upright bass"), [steel guitar](/wiki/Steel_guitar "Steel guitar"), [banjo](/wiki/Banjo "Banjo"), [dobro](/wiki/Dobro "Dobro"), piano, keyboards, saxophone, [harmonica](/wiki/Harmonica "Harmonica"), [fiddle](/wiki/Fiddle "Fiddle"), and drums.[[6]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-7) In 2020, Williams Jr. was inducted into the [Country Music Hall of Fame](/wiki/Country_Music_Hall_of_Fame_and_Museum "Country Music Hall of Fame and Museum").[[7]](#cite_note-Paulson-8)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hank_Williams_Jr.&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Randall Hank Williams was born on May 26, 1949, in [Shreveport, Louisiana](/wiki/Shreveport,_Louisiana "Shreveport, Louisiana"). His [father](/wiki/Hank_Williams "Hank Williams") nicknamed him Bocephus (after [Grand Ole Opry](/wiki/Grand_Ole_Opry "Grand Ole Opry") comedian [Rod Brasfield](/wiki/Rod_Brasfield "Rod Brasfield")'s ventriloquist dummy).[[8]](#cite_note-9) After his father's death in 1953, he was raised by his mother, [Audrey Williams](/wiki/Audrey_Williams "Audrey Williams").

While he was a child, Williams was influenced by a number of contemporary musicians who visited his family and taught him various musical instruments and styles. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Among these figures of influence were [Merle Haggard](/wiki/Merle_Haggard "Merle Haggard"), [Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash"), [Fats Domino](/wiki/Fats_Domino "Fats Domino"), [Earl Scruggs](/wiki/Earl_Scruggs "Earl Scruggs"), [Lightnin' Hopkins](/wiki/Lightnin%27_Hopkins "Lightnin' Hopkins"), and [Jerry Lee Lewis](/wiki/Jerry_Lee_Lewis "Jerry Lee Lewis"). Williams first stepped onto the stage and sang his father's songs when he was eight years old.

He attended [John Overton High School](/wiki/John_Overton_High_School "John Overton High School") in [Nashville, Tennessee](/wiki/Nashville,_Tennessee "Nashville, Tennessee"), where he would take his guitar to music class and play for pep rallies and performances with the choir.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hank_Williams_Jr.&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

In 1964, Williams made his recording debut with "[Long Gone Lonesome Blues](/wiki/Long_Gone_Lonesome_Blues "Long Gone Lonesome Blues")", one of his father's many classic songs.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

He provided the singing voice of his father[[10]](#cite_note-pc9-11) in the 1964 film *[Your Cheatin' Heart](/wiki/Your_Cheatin%27_Heart_(film) "Your Cheatin' Heart (film)").*[[11]](#cite_note-12) He also recorded an album of duets with recordings of his father.[[10]](#cite_note-pc9-11)

### A change in appearance and musical direction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hank_Williams_Jr.&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: A change in appearance and musical direction")]

Although Williams' recordings earned him numerous country hits throughout the 1960s and early 1970s with his role as a "Hank Williams impersonator", he became disillusioned and severed ties with his mother.

By the mid-1970s Williams began to pursue a musical direction that would eventually make him a superstar. While recording a series of moderately successful songs, Williams began a pattern of heavy drug and alcohol abuse. Upon moving to [Alabama](/wiki/Alabama "Alabama"), in an attempt to refocus both his creative energy and his troubled personal life, Williams began playing music with [Southern rock](/wiki/Southern_rock "Southern rock") and [Outlaw country](/wiki/Outlaw_country "Outlaw country") musicians including [Waylon Jennings](/wiki/Waylon_Jennings "Waylon Jennings"), [Toy Caldwell](/wiki/Toy_Caldwell "Toy Caldwell"), and [Charlie Daniels](/wiki/Charlie_Daniels "Charlie Daniels"). *[Hank Williams Jr. and Friends](/wiki/Hank_Williams_Jr._and_Friends "Hank Williams Jr. and Friends")* (1975), often considered his watershed album, was the product of these then-groundbreaking collaborations.

On August 8, 1975, Williams was nearly killed while mountain climbing in southwestern [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana"). While climbing [Ajax Peak](/wiki/List_of_mountains_in_Beaverhead_County,_Montana "List of mountains in Beaverhead County, Montana") on the [continental divide](/wiki/Continental_Divide_of_the_Americas "Continental Divide of the Americas") ([Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho") border) west of [Jackson](/wiki/Jackson,_Montana "Jackson, Montana"), the snow beneath Williams collapsed and he fell almost 500 feet (150 m) onto rock, causing multiple severe skull and facial fractures.[[12]](#cite_note-singfll-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14) Williams spent two years recovering, re-learning how to talk and sing, and undergoing 17 surgeries to repair his skull and reconstruct his face. The accident was chronicled in the semi-autobiographical, made-for-television film *[Living Proof: The Hank Williams Jr. Story](/wiki/Living_Proof:_The_Hank_Williams_Jr._Story "Living Proof: The Hank Williams Jr. Story")*. To hide his scars and disfigurement from the accident, Williams grew a beard and began wearing sunglasses and a [cowboy hat](/wiki/Cowboy_hat "Cowboy hat"). The beard, hat, and sunglasses have since become Williams' [signature look](/wiki/Trademark_look "Trademark look").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1977, Williams recorded and released *One Night Stands* and *The New South*, and worked closely with his old friend [Waylon Jennings](/wiki/Waylon_Jennings "Waylon Jennings") on the song "Once and For All".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1980, he appeared on the [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") show *[Austin City Limits](/wiki/Austin_City_Limits "Austin City Limits")* during Season 5, along with the Shake Russell-[Dana Cooper](/wiki/Dana_Cooper "Dana Cooper") Band.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Country music career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hank_Williams_Jr.&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Country music career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/HankWilliamsJrCollage-1000.jpg/500px-HankWilliamsJrCollage-1000.jpg)](/wiki/File:HankWilliamsJrCollage-1000.jpg)

Williams performing at the Chumash Casino Resort in Santa Ynez, California, 2006

In 1976, *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* wrote that Williams' "mainstream country material has always been among [Nashville](/wiki/Nashville_Tennessee "Nashville Tennessee")'s best".[[14]](#cite_note-15)

He was prolific throughout the 1980s, sometimes recording and releasing two albums a year. *[Family Tradition](/wiki/Family_Tradition_(album) "Family Tradition (album)")*, *[Whiskey Bent and Hell Bound](/wiki/Whiskey_Bent_and_Hell_Bound_(album) "Whiskey Bent and Hell Bound (album)")*, *[Habits Old and New](/wiki/Habits_Old_and_New "Habits Old and New")*, *[Rowdy](/wiki/Rowdy_(Hank_Williams,_Jr._album) "Rowdy (Hank Williams, Jr. album)")*, *[The Pressure Is On](/wiki/The_Pressure_Is_On "The Pressure Is On")*, *[High Notes](/wiki/High_Notes "High Notes")*, *[Strong Stuff](/wiki/Strong_Stuff "Strong Stuff")*, *[Man of Steel](/wiki/Man_of_Steel_(album) "Man of Steel (album)")*, *[Major Moves](/wiki/Major_Moves_(album) "Major Moves (album)")*, *[Five-O](/wiki/Five-O_(album) "Five-O (album)")*, *[Montana Cafe](/wiki/Montana_Cafe "Montana Cafe")*, and many others resulted in a long string of hits.

Between 1979 and 1992, Williams released 21 albums—18 studio albums and three compilations—that were all certified at least [gold](/wiki/Music_recording_certification "Music recording certification") by the [RIAA](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America"). Between 1979 and 1990, he enjoyed a string of 30 Top Ten singles on the Billboard Country charts, including eight No. 1 singles, for a total of 44 Top Ten singles, including a total of 10 No. 1 singles, during his career.

In 1982, he had nine albums simultaneously on the Billboard Top Country Albums chart, all of which were original works and not compilations. In 1987–88, Williams was named Entertainer of the Year by the [Country Music Association](/wiki/Country_Music_Association "Country Music Association"). In 1987, 1988, and 1989, he won the same award from the [Academy of Country Music](/wiki/Academy_of_Country_Music "Academy of Country Music"). The pinnacle album of his acceptance and popularity was *[Born to Boogie](/wiki/Born_to_Boogie_(album) "Born to Boogie (album)")*.

During the 1980s, Williams Jr. became a country music superstar known for catchy anthems and hard-edged, rock-influenced country. During the late 1970s and into the mid-1980s, Williams' songs constantly flew into the number one or number two spots, with songs such as "Family Tradition", "Whiskey Bent and Hell Bound", "Old Habits", "Ain't Misbehavin'", "[Born to Boogie](/wiki/Born_to_Boogie_(song) "Born to Boogie (song)")", and "My Name Is Bocephus".

The hit single "[Wild Streak](/wiki/Wild_Streak "Wild Streak")" (1987) was co-written by [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston") native [Terri Sharp](/wiki/Terri_Sharp "Terri Sharp"), for which Williams and Sharp both earned gold records. In 1988, he released a Southern pride song, "[If the South Woulda Won](/wiki/If_the_South_Woulda_Won "If the South Woulda Won")". The reference is to a notional Southern victory in the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War").

His 1989 hit "[There's a Tear in My Beer](/wiki/There%27s_a_Tear_in_My_Beer "There's a Tear in My Beer")" was a duet with his father created using electronic merging technology. The song was written by his father, and had been previously recorded with Hank Williams playing the guitar as the sole instrument. The music video for the song combined existing television footage of Hank Williams performing, onto which electronic merging technology impressed the recordings of Williams, which then made it appear as if he were actually playing with his father. The video was both a critical and commercial success. It was named *Video of the Year* by both the Country Music Association and the Academy of Country Music. Williams would go on to win a [Grammy Award](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award") in 1990 for [Best Country Vocal Collaboration](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Country_Collaboration_with_Vocals "Grammy Award for Best Country Collaboration with Vocals").

He is well known for his hit "[A Country Boy Can Survive](/wiki/A_Country_Boy_Can_Survive "A Country Boy Can Survive")" and as the performer of the theme song for *[Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football")*, based on his 1984 hit "[All My Rowdy Friends Are Coming Over Tonight](/wiki/All_My_Rowdy_Friends_Are_Coming_Over_Tonight "All My Rowdy Friends Are Coming Over Tonight")". In 1991, 1992, 1993, and 1994, Williams' opening themes for *Monday Night Football* earned him four [Emmy Awards](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Hank_Williams%2C_Jr._%282006%29.jpg/250px-Hank_Williams%2C_Jr._%282006%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hank_Williams,_Jr._(2006).jpg)

Williams in 2006

In 2000, he provided the voice of Injun Joe in *[Tom Sawyer](/wiki/Tom_Sawyer_(2000_film) "Tom Sawyer (2000 film)")*. In 2001, Williams Jr. co-wrote his classic hit "A Country Boy Can Survive" after 9/11, renaming it "America Can Survive". In 2004, Williams was featured prominently on *CMT Outlaws*. In 2006, he starred at the Summerfest concert.

He has also made a cameo appearance along with [Larry the Cable Guy](/wiki/Larry_the_Cable_Guy "Larry the Cable Guy"), [Kid Rock](/wiki/Kid_Rock "Kid Rock"), and [Charlie Daniels](/wiki/Charlie_Daniels "Charlie Daniels") in [Gretchen Wilson](/wiki/Gretchen_Wilson "Gretchen Wilson")'s music video for the song "[All Jacked Up](/wiki/All_Jacked_Up_(song) "All Jacked Up (song)")". He and Kid Rock also appeared in Wilson's "[Redneck Woman](/wiki/Redneck_Woman "Redneck Woman")" video. Hank also had a small part of Kid Rock's video "Only God Knows Why", and "Redneck Paradise".

In April 2009, Williams released a new single, "Red, White & Pink-Slip Blues", which peaked at number 43 on the country charts. The song was the lead-off single to Williams' album *[127 Rose Avenue](/wiki/127_Rose_Avenue "127 Rose Avenue")*. The album debuted and peaked at number 7 on the *Billboard* Top Country Albums chart. Also in July 2009, *127 Rose Avenue* was announced as his last album for Curb Records.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

## Musical style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hank_Williams_Jr.&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Musical style")]

As a multi-instrumentalist, Williams' repertoire of skills includes guitar, bass guitar, [upright bass](/wiki/Upright_bass "Upright bass"), [steel guitar](/wiki/Steel_guitar "Steel guitar"), [banjo](/wiki/Banjo "Banjo"), [Resonator guitar](/wiki/Resonator_guitar "Resonator guitar"), piano, keyboards, saxophone, [harmonica](/wiki/Harmonica "Harmonica"), [fiddle](/wiki/Fiddle "Fiddle"), and drums.[[6]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-7) Williams began his recording career performing covers of his father's songs. Despite catering to the country music market, Williams preferred to listen to [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues").[[16]](#cite_note-Dukes-17) Williams also recorded singles under the name Luke the Drifter Jr.[[17]](#cite_note-Harrington-18) (a reference to his father's alias "Luke the Drifter"), [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") singles under the aliases Rockin' Randall[[18]](#cite_note-19) and Bocephus[[17]](#cite_note-Harrington-18) (a nickname given to him by his father), and [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") under the name Thunderhead Hawkins.[[16]](#cite_note-Dukes-17) Williams' music has been categorized as [country rock](/wiki/Country_rock "Country rock"),[[5]](#cite_note-Burns-6) [blues rock](/wiki/Blues_rock "Blues rock"),[[19]](#cite_note-Webb-20) [southern rock](/wiki/Southern_rock "Southern rock"),[[20]](#cite_note-Malone-21) [outlaw country](/wiki/Outlaw_country "Outlaw country"),[[21]](#cite_note-Erlewine-22) [rockabilly](/wiki/Rockabilly "Rockabilly")[[19]](#cite_note-Webb-20) and [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll").[[19]](#cite_note-Webb-20)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hank_Williams_Jr.&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Legacy")]

Artists who have cited Hank Williams Jr. as an influence include Delta Generators,[[22]](#cite_note-23) [Walker Hayes](/wiki/Walker_Hayes "Walker Hayes"),[[23]](#cite_note-24) [Sam Hunt](/wiki/Sam_Hunt "Sam Hunt"),[[24]](#cite_note-25) Davin James,[[25]](#cite_note-26) [Shooter Jennings](/wiki/Shooter_Jennings "Shooter Jennings"),[[26]](#cite_note-27) [Wayne Mills](/wiki/Wayne_Mills_(singer) "Wayne Mills (singer)"),[[27]](#cite_note-28) The Sickstring Outlaws[[28]](#cite_note-29) and [Gretchen Wilson](/wiki/Gretchen_Wilson "Gretchen Wilson").[[29]](#cite_note-30)

On April 10, 2006, [CMT](/wiki/Country_Music_Television "Country Music Television") honored Williams with the [Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash") Visionary Award, presenting it to him at the 2006 [CMT Music Awards](/wiki/CMT_Music_Awards "CMT Music Awards"). On November 11, 2008, Williams was honored as a [BMI](/wiki/Broadcast_Music_Incorporated "Broadcast Music Incorporated") Icon at the 56th annual BMI Country Awards. The artists and songwriters named BMI Icons have had "a unique and indelible influence on generations of music makers".[[30]](#cite_note-31)

In 2015, Williams was inducted into the Louisiana Music Hall of Fame. On August 12, 2020, Williams was selected to be inducted into the [Country Music Hall of Fame](/wiki/Country_Music_Hall_of_Fame_and_Museum "Country Music Hall of Fame and Museum").[[7]](#cite_note-Paulson-8)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hank_Williams_Jr.&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Personal life")]

His daughter Katherine Williams-Dunning, the only one of his five children to not pursue a music career, died in a car crash on June 13, 2020, at age 27.[[31]](#cite_note-32) His son Shelton performs as [Hank Williams III](/wiki/Hank_Williams_III "Hank Williams III"). His other children include [Holly Williams](/wiki/Holly_Williams_(musician) "Holly Williams (musician)")[[32]](#cite_note-33) who is also a musician, [Sam Williams](/wiki/Sam_Williams_(singer) "Sam Williams (singer)"), also a musician,[[33]](#cite_note-34) as is his grandson [Coleman Williams](/wiki/Coleman_Williams "Coleman Williams") (Hank III's son), who performs under the sobriquet "IV". His wife Mary Jane Thomas died on March 22, 2022, aged 58, after complications from a medical procedure.[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36) On September 9, 2023, Williams married his long-time friend since 2003, Brandi. The couple became engaged on Mother's Day earlier that year.[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hank_Williams_Jr.&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Politics")]

Williams is a registered [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") and been politically involved with the party for decades. In the [2000 U.S. presidential election](/wiki/2000_U.S._Presidential_election "2000 U.S. Presidential election"), he rerecorded his song "We Are Young Country" to "This is Bush-Cheney Country". On October 15, 2008, at a rally in [Virginia Beach](/wiki/Virginia_Beach,_Virginia "Virginia Beach, Virginia") for Republican presidential nominee [John McCain](/wiki/John_McCain "John McCain"), he performed "McCain-Palin Tradition", a song in support of McCain and his running mate, [Sarah Palin](/wiki/Sarah_Palin "Sarah Palin").[[38]](#cite_note-39) He has contributed to federal election campaigns, mostly to Republicans, including [Michele Bachmann](/wiki/Michele_Bachmann "Michele Bachmann")'s 2012 presidential campaign. However, he has donated to some Democrats in the past, most notably [Jim Cooper](/wiki/Jim_Cooper "Jim Cooper") and [John S. Tanner](/wiki/John_S._Tanner "John S. Tanner").[[39]](#cite_note-40)

In November 2008, Williams considered a run for the 2012 Republican nomination as a [U.S. Senator](/wiki/U.S._Senator "U.S. Senator") from Tennessee for the seat held by GOP incumbent [Bob Corker](/wiki/Bob_Corker "Bob Corker").[[40]](#cite_note-41) Williams ultimately decided against it.

Williams declined to endorse a candidate in the [2016 Republican primary](/wiki/2016_Republican_Party_presidential_primaries "2016 Republican Party presidential primaries"). However, he has since voiced support for [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), and attended an [Alabama Crimson Tide football](/wiki/Alabama_Crimson_Tide_football "Alabama Crimson Tide football") game alongside Trump and [Kid Rock](/wiki/Kid_Rock "Kid Rock") in September 2024.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

### 2011 *Fox and Friends* controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hank_Williams_Jr.&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2011 Fox and Friends controversy")]

In an October 3, 2011, interview with [Fox News Channel](/wiki/Fox_News_Channel "Fox News Channel")'s *[Fox & Friends](/wiki/Fox_%26_Friends "Fox & Friends")*, Williams discussed a June golf game where President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") and Republican House Speaker [John Boehner](/wiki/John_Boehner "John Boehner") had teamed against Vice President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") and Ohio Governor [John Kasich](/wiki/John_Kasich "John Kasich"), saying the match was "one of the biggest political mistakes ever". When asked why the golf game troubled him, Williams stated, "Come on. That'd be like [Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") playing golf with [Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu") ... in the shape this country is in?" He also said that the President and Vice President were "the enemy" and compared them to "the [Three Stooges](/wiki/Three_Stooges "Three Stooges")". Later, anchor [Gretchen Carlson](/wiki/Gretchen_Carlson "Gretchen Carlson") said to him, "You used the name of one of the most hated people in all of the world to describe, *I think*, the president." Williams replied, "Well, that is true. But I'm telling you like it is." As a result of his statements, [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") dropped Williams' opening song from its *[Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football")* broadcast of the [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers") versus the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") and replaced it with Ken Block and Andrew Copeland of [Sister Hazel](/wiki/Sister_Hazel "Sister Hazel") singing the [national anthem](/wiki/The_Star-Spangled_Banner "The Star-Spangled Banner").[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44)

Williams later said his analogy was "extreme – but it was to make a point", and "some of us have strong opinions and are often misunderstood ... I was simply trying to explain how stupid it seemed to me – how ludicrous that pairing was. They're polar opposites, and it made no sense. They don't see eye to eye and never will". Additionally, Williams said he has "always respected the office of the president ... Working-class people are hurting – and it doesn't seem like anybody cares. When both sides are high-fiving it on the ninth hole when everybody else is without a job – it makes a whole lot of us angry. Something has to change. The policies have to change". ESPN later said it was "extremely disappointed" in Williams' comments, and pulled his opening from that night's broadcast.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

Three days later, ESPN announced Williams and his song would not return to *Monday Night Football*, ending the use of the song that had been part of the broadcast on both ABC and ESPN since 1989.[[45]](#cite_note-46) Williams expressed defiance and indifference on his website, and said he was the one who had made the decision. "After reading hundreds of e-mails, I have made MY decision," he wrote. "By pulling my opening Oct 3rd, You (ESPN) stepped on the Toes of The First Amendment Freedom of Speech, so therefore Me, My Song, and All My Rowdy Friends are OUT OF HERE. It's been a great run."[[46]](#cite_note-47) Williams' son, [Hank Williams III](/wiki/Hank_Williams_III "Hank Williams III"), stayed neutral in the debate, telling [TMZ.com](/wiki/TMZ.com "TMZ.com") that most musicians, including his father, are "not worthy" of a political discussion.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

After his song was pulled from *Monday Night Football,* Williams recorded a song criticizing Obama, ESPN and *[Fox & Friends](/wiki/Fox_%26_Friends "Fox & Friends")*, titled "[Keep the Change](/wiki/Keep_the_Change_(Hank_Williams_Jr._song) "Keep the Change (Hank Williams Jr. song)")". He released the track on [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes") and via free download at his website.[[48]](#cite_note-49) The song garnered over 180,000 downloads in two days.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

Williams continued to make his opinions of Obama known and during a performance at the Iowa State Fair in August 2012, [he called Obama a Muslim](/wiki/Barack_Obama_religion_conspiracy_theories "Barack Obama religion conspiracy theories") telling the crowd, "We've got a Muslim president who hates farming, hates the military, hates the U.S. and we hate him!"[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52)
